name: Yellow wax bean (food medium)

components.name: water
components.evidence: Standard reference value; matrix is ~90% water

components.name: total protein
components.evidence: Nitrogen x 6.25

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: VandenLangenberg et al. 2012 Crop Science doi:10.2135/cropsci2011.07.0396 (range 0.25-1.78 g/100g FW); USDA FDC 169320 total sugars 3.27
components.evidence: Fructose is typically the dominant free sugar in snap-bean pods; midpoint of measured fresh-weight range

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: VandenLangenberg et al. 2012 doi:10.2135/cropsci2011.07.0396 (range 0.23-1.38 g/100g FW)
components.evidence: Second-most-abundant free sugar; fructose >= glucose > sucrose pattern

components.name: sucrose
components.source: VandenLangenberg et al. 2012 doi:10.2135/cropsci2011.07.0396 (range 0.10-0.78 g/100g FW); FooDB FOOD00882 lists sucrose
components.evidence: Minor disaccharide; increases with pod maturity

components.name: myo-inositol
components.source: Sanchez-Mata et al. 2002 Eur Food Res Technol doi:10.1007/s00217-001-0447-0 (soluble sugars in green beans by HPLC)
components.evidence: Identified as a soluble polyol in green-bean pods; not individually quantified for the yellow cultivar

components.name: total sugars
components.evidence: Total sugars; exceeds sum of individually reported mono/disaccharides due to cultivar/maturity variation and polyol contribution

components.name: dietary fiber
components.evidence: Largely insoluble; not a rapidly fermentable carbon source

components.name: total carbohydrate
components.evidence: By difference

components.name: L-malic acid
components.source: Gonzalez-Castro, Oruna-Concha, Lopez-Hernandez & Simal-Lozano 1997 Z Lebensm Unters Forsch A 204:365-368 doi:10.1007/s002170050091 (fresh P. vulgaris var. Helda pods, HPLC)
components.evidence: PREDOMINANT organic acid of fresh green/snap-bean pods; a major C4 carbon/energy source that was entirely missing from the FooDB profile. Titratable acidity (as malic) of fresh pods = 613 mg/100g

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence: Third-ranked acid after malic and oxalic in fresh pods, HPLC; contributes to pod tartness

components.name: oxalic acid
components.evidence: Second-most-abundant acid by HPLC (total oxalic); method-sensitive - aggregator soluble-oxalate values are lower (22-65 mg/100g). Decreases with freezing/blanching

components.name: fumaric acid
components.evidence: Minor TCA-cycle acid, trace level in green-bean pods, HPLC

components.name: L-ascorbate (vitamin C)
components.evidence: Ascorbic acid; also an organic acid

components.name: folate (B9)
components.evidence: Total folate

components.name: phylloquinone (vitamin K1)
components.evidence: Phytomenadione

components.name: beta-carotene (provitamin A)
components.evidence: Yellow wax cultivar: carotene content is negligible (chlorophyll/carotenoid-poor pod), unlike green snap beans; kept but low/near-zero

components.name: phosphorus
components.evidence: Available as phosphate for growth

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: Major cation

components.name: molybdenum
components.evidence: Genuine plant micronutrient (nitrate reductase/molybdoenzyme cofactor); not quantified for this food

components.name: nickel
components.evidence: Genuine plant micronutrient (urease cofactor in legumes); trace, unquantified

components.name: cobalt
components.evidence: Trace element associated with legume N-fixation symbiont; kept at trace

components.name: L-aspartate
components.evidence: Protein-bound profile; aspartate/asparagine is the dominant amino acid of bean pods

components.name: L-cystine
components.evidence: Reported as cystine (cysteine dimer)

components.name: L-citrulline
components.evidence: Genuine free non-protein amino acid in Phaseolus; unquantified

components.name: L-glutamine
components.evidence: Free amide amino acid; kept

components.name: phosphate
components.evidence: Medium inorganic ion (from phosphorus pool)

components.name: sulfate
components.evidence: Medium inorganic ion / sulfur source

components.name: chloride
components.evidence: Medium inorganic ion

components.name: ammonium
components.evidence: Reduced-N source for the model

remove.name: Creatine cytosol
remove.reason: Animal muscle metabolite; not synthesized by Phaseolus; FooDB predicted/implausible for a plant food

remove.name: Taurine C2H7NO3S
remove.reason: Animal-derived amino sulfonic acid; essentially absent in plants; FooDB predicted

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed (animal) vitamin A; USDA vitamin A RAE = 0 for yellow wax bean; plants supply provitamin-A carotenoids, not retinol; FooDB artifact

remove.name: Racemethionine
remove.reason: Synthetic DL-methionine; not a natural food constituent (natural form is L-methionine, already listed); FooDB noise

remove.name: L-Methionine Sulfoxide
remove.reason: Oxidation artifact of methionine, not a genuine nutrient of the fresh pod

remove.name: NG,NG-Dimethyl-L-arginine
remove.reason: ADMA is a mammalian NOS-pathway metabolite; not a plant growth nutrient; FooDB predicted

remove.name: Histamine
remove.reason: Trace biogenic amine, predicted; not a growth-relevant nutrient of fresh bean pods

missing_added: L-malic acid (286 mg/100g) - PREDOMINANT organic acid, quantified; absent from FooDB
individual free sugars with amounts: D-fructose, D-glucose, sucrose
quantitative full mineral panel (Ca, Fe, Mg, P, K, Na, Zn, Cu, Mn, Se)
keep_uncovered: L-DOPA / dopamine (trace catecholamines; low confidence for Phaseolus pods)
notes: Yellow wax bean = yellow-podded cultivar of common snap bean (Phaseolus vulgaris); full macro/amino-acid/vitamin/mineral panel is high-confidence from USDA FDC 169320 (Beans, snap, yellow, raw). Dominant fermentable carbon sources are the monosaccharides fructose and glucose (fructose >= glucose > sucrose) plus the previously-missing organic acids - chiefly malic (286 mg/100g, ~21 mM) and oxalic (191 mg/100g, ~21 mM), with minor citric (39.9 mg/100g) and trace fumaric. These organic acids were the main FooDB omission and are now quantified from Gonzalez-Castro et al. 1997 (doi:10.1007/s002170050091, fresh var. Helda pods, HPLC); oxalic is the most method-sensitive value. Quinic acid is deliberately NOT included: that study explicitly detected quinic acid only in the co-studied peppers, not in green beans. Yellow cultivar carotene is negligible (vitamin A RAE = 0). Low-fat, moderate-protein (1.8 g/100g), potassium-dominant mineral matrix.
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 169320 (Beans, snap, yellow, raw)
VandenLangenberg, Bethke & Nienhuis 2012, Crop Science 52:1692, doi:10.2135/cropsci2011.07.0396 (fructose/glucose/sucrose in snap-bean pods)
VandenLangenberg et al. 2012, HortScience 47:874 (sugar accumulation patterns in snap/dry bean pods)
Sanchez-Mata et al. 2002, Eur Food Res Technol, doi:10.1007/s00217-001-0447-0 (soluble sugars in green beans by HPLC)

Gonzalez-Castro, Oruna-Concha, Lopez-Hernandez & Simal-Lozano 1997, Z Lebensm Unters Forsch A 204:365-368, doi:10.1007/s002170050091 (quantified fresh green-bean organic acids: malic 286, oxalic 191, citric 39.9, fumaric 1.09 mg/100g; quinic not detected)
Vazquez Oderiz et al. 1994, J AOAC Int 77:1056, PMID 8069112 (HPLC method for organic acids + vitamin C in green beans)
